title: ‘The meaning of poverty' and contemporary quantitative poverty research
abstract: ‘. . . the study of poverty has not developed theoretically during the course of this century’ (Townsend 2010 [1962]: 93 [218]). During the postwar period, policymakers and social scientists alike began to believe that the problem of poverty was nearly solved (Bradshaw 2000). The twin engines of welfare state expansion coupled with economic growth would cushion the experience of economic marginality, while rising living standards should ensure a more enjoyable quality of life for the vast majority of the populace. No more starvation in the streets; no more rank desperation for those unsuccessful in the marketplace. Poverty, as a social condition, would be a social artifact of the past, and of far-away places, much like polio today. Townsend's article (2010 [1962]) challenges this rosy prediction on theoretical and empirical grounds. Theoretically, Townsend seeks to recast social scientists' conception of poverty away from the fixed subsistence standards utilized in the classic British poverty studies. Instead, Townsend argues for a relative conception of poverty that captures poverty's ‘dynamic’ nature (2010 [l962]: 93 [218]). Poverty, for Townsend, cannot be divorced from the standards of living within actual communities at a particular point in time. Should these standards change, our conceptualization of poverty must change as well. Poor individuals in the present day don't look well on their poverty given the struggles of their long-dead forebears, nor does the lack of a vehicle today connote the same thing as it did fifty years prior, especially if the nearest job opportunity sits sixty kilometers away. Thus poverty as a social condition must be defined in reference to the period in which an individual lives. Yet as Townsend first articulates in ‘The meaning of poverty’ and then expands on in subsequent works, poverty also must be understood as locally defined according to the norms predominating in particular communities, allowing for comparative analyses of poverty that adjust for ‘differences in condition between different societies at a simultaneous moment in time’ (Townsend 1985: 660). Poverty in Bangladesh looks, feels, and acts differently from poverty in Southampton. For many social scientists today, such an assertion rings of the obvious. Yet it should not: those British politicians and policymakers championing the end of poverty during the ‘golden age’ of welfare capitalism were comparing postwar British living standards to British standards of prior periods, and to living standards in other places. Nor are such comparisons – comparisons that imply an absolute standard of poverty – confined to the immediate postwar years: witness John Moore in 1989, Secretary of State for Social Security, famously declaring that poverty no longer had meaning in the contemporary British context since absolute deprivation had been (nearly) eliminated (speech cited in Bradshaw 2000: 55). The resistance to a relative rather than absolute standard of poverty continues among many policymakers, much of the public, and media figures (Cox and Alm 1999; Lawson 2006). The USA still adheres to government-sanctioned absolute poverty thresholds first established in the early 1960s that remain largely unadjusted except for inflation. The official endorsement of an outdated, absolute measure of poverty persists despite decades of criticism by social scientists, activists, and (select) policymakers. Indeed, as far back as the publication of ‘The meaning of poverty’, Harrington critiqued absolute measures of poverty in favor of a definition sensitive to prevailing standards of the society in question (1962).2 However, today a ‘rising consensus’ of social scientists supports a relative understanding of poverty for studies of the advanced capitalist democracies (Brady 2003: 719; see also Heuveline and Weinshenker 2008: 176). This growing agreement among academics is testament to the enormous theoretical importance of Townsend's 1962 piece and his subsequent research on the issue. And as a result, nearly half a century following publication, our theoretical understanding of poverty has expanded dramatically. This is not to claim that the influence of Townsend's article and his subsequent theorizing on poverty was immediate, absolute, or ultimately decisive. In the following discussion, I highlight where Townsend's agenda for poverty research has fallen short, and provide a pathway for future research consistent with the main thrust of Townsend's theory of poverty. We must remember that to some extent the concept of ‘poverty’ is independent of that of income. (Townsend 2010 [1962]: 98 [223]) On the empirical side, progress on poverty research has been uneven. Easily overlooked given the rich theoretical contribution of ‘The meaning of poverty’ is Townsend's important empirical contribution to the study of economic deprivation. Critiquing the prior efforts of Rowntree and others to establish subsistence standards – standards devised by researchers endeavoring to capture the nutritional requirements of modern existence – Townsend instead argues for surveying how poor or near-poor persons actually get by in their daily lives (surveys he consistently carried out in collaboration with colleagues. For a recent example, see Townsend et al. 1997). This refocus on the actual living conditions of individuals stems from Townsend's insistence of a theory of poverty situated in the ‘conventions and resources of any particular society which we happen to be studying’ (Townsend 2010 [1962]: 95 [220]). It simply makes no sense to calculate the average caloric intake of an average individual disconnected from the social setting in which that individual resides. Not only might the foodstuffs required to meet daily needs not be available in particular locales, those needs themselves are ‘essentially social’, and therefore vary over time and across place (Townsend 1985: 667). Yet four decades following publication of ‘The meaning of poverty’ a review of the state of poverty scholarship in the US concluded that ‘the measurement of poverty has not received the scrutiny it deserves’ (Brady 2003: 716).3 In 1963, Mollie Orshansky, then a researcher in President Johnson's Social Security Administration, devised a economic deprivation measure based on US family consumption data from the mid-1950s (Wilson 1991). For generations now, the US government has relied on the income thresholds that stem directly from Orshansky's measure to determine poverty rates and trends in the USA, despite drastic changes in family budget allocations, changes in living standards and arrangements, and wide geographic variation in costs of living. Among US scholars, critiques of the official measure are now so commonplace as to become almost cliché (for a few notable examples, see Betson and Warlick 1998; Ruggles 1990; Wilson 1991). Nevertheless, a survey of major US poverty studies published during the last decade of the twentieth Century found that over two-thirds still rely on the official US measure (Brady 2003: Table 3). The USA – thankfully – remains an outlier in this case, as many European governments have moved toward a relative poverty definition, and many poverty scholars not focusing exclusively on the USA likewise eschew absolute measures of deprivation (Brady 2003; 2005; Heuveline and Weinshenker 2008; Kenworthy 1999; Smeeding, Rainwater, and Burtless 2001). Still the USA remains a large country, with a large number of poverty scholars, and – no matter how poverty is operationalized – a large proportion of the developed world's poor. More importantly, the difficulties associated with measuring poverty in the way Townsend originally theorizes the concept go well beyond simply adopting a relative threshold. Townsend asserts that ‘those individuals and families whose resources . . . fall seriously short’ of the resources enjoyed by a typical individual or family in the particular community under study live in poverty (Townsend 2010 [1962]: 99 [225]). Defining ‘seriously short’ has proven exceptionally tricky. Recent data collection efforts – especially the Luxembourg Income Study (LIS) – have sparked a large body of research that adopts relative poverty measures standardized to ensure comparability across countries and over time (for working papers associated with the LIS, see http://www.lisproject.org/publications/wpapers.htm). Often, researchers capture a nation's poverty level for a particular year by the percentage of the population with annual incomes less than 50 per cent of the median for that year. Is less than half the median ‘seriously short’ of the resources necessary to participate fully in the life of the community? Without repeated surveys of those individuals and families with annual incomes near this ‘essentially arbitrary cutoff point’ we simply do not know (Heuveline and Weinshenker 2008: 176; see also Callan and Nolan 1991). Other objections to such frequently used relative measures are well known, such as their ‘deceptive’ behaviour during periods of serious recession (when a recession can produce a drastic decline in relative poverty rates over the short term) (Iceland 2005: 211; Sen 1983). But problems associated with measuring poverty in a way consistent with Townsend's theory extend even beyond those commonly covered in the poverty literature. As Townsend and others repeatedly emphasize, poverty is not to be confused with inequality (Brady 2003; Osberg and Xu 2000; Townsend 1985). Instead, poverty connotes a threshold effect: below some minimum level of resources, the experience of deprivation intensifies to the point where the individual is ‘excluded from ordinary living patterns, customs, and activities’ (Townsend 1979: 660). Specifying where that threshold lies – where the quite commonplace experience of economic insecurity shifts suddenly to an experience in which a family is ‘excluded from ordinary living patterns, customs and activities’– is a demanding empirical task. Mostly, we social scientists know poverty when we see it. We have not mastered the ability to measure where it starts in our large-scale surveys. In ‘The meaning of poverty’Townsend calls for measures of poverty that expand well-beyond pre-tax annual incomes (2010 [1962]: 96–7 [221–3]; 1980). If poverty describes a state of social exclusion, then simple wage and salary estimates represent a coarse and misleading measure of such a multi-dimensional concept. On this front, there has been meaningful progress. Leading poverty research today frequently includes various measures of taxes and state transfers (Brady 2005; Moller et al. 2003). Meanwhile, the recent growth of the ‘social exclusion’ literature represents another fruitful avenue toward Townsend's own understanding of poverty. Whereas relative poverty indicators that centre around market and state-mediated income usually ‘assume differences in participation as a result of economic deprivation’ (Roosa et al. 2005), indicators of exclusion directly tap into the lack of societal participation characteristic of Townsend's notion of poverty. The operationalization of exclusion varies widely, but all exclusion measures attempt to measure the lack of capacity to participate fully in one's community. Combined with more comprehensive indicators of economic resources, these exclusion variables represent a crucial step forward in capturing an understanding of poverty consistent with Townsend's theory. This proliferation of poverty measures, however, often leads to conceptual confusion and conflicting findings in the social science literature. Depending on the measure chosen, the percentage poor in a particular country and cross-national poverty trends can vary substantially (Atkinson 1998; Iceland 2005: Figure 2). Given Townsend's own multi-faceted understanding of poverty, we should not expect the field to coalesce around one single indicator. This lack of a consensus measure is justifiable so long as we do a better job linking our measures of poverty to the mechanisms our theories predict connect poverty to other social phenomena. Imagine a poor individual who fails to vote in the upcoming national elections. She may not vote for various reasons related to her impoverished status. She may live in a rural community with sparse polling places – lacking any means of transportation, the costs of getting herself to the polls could be prohibitively high. In a society marked by high levels of inequality, she and her fellow impoverished citizens may be turned off by a political landscape in which the relatively rich dominate by systematically excluding issues of interest to the economically marginal from political debate (Schattschneider 1960; Solt 2008). Or the individual may be literally excluded from voting – as is true for certain classes of criminals in many US states (Manza and Uggen 2006). These examples represent various theories that link a relative conception of poverty to depressed political participation among the poor. Each implies a distinct measure of poverty, however: The first is best captured by a resource deprivation measure tapping into an individual's transportation needs; the second by a relative poverty measure incorporating inequality; the third by an indicator of social exclusion. A crude income-based measure of poverty may count this individual as poor; it may not. Poverty is a multi-faceted concept measurable – and now frequently measured – in various ways. Yet too often our measures of poverty remain disconnected with our theories of what causes poverty or what effects stem from poverty. Moving the field forward requires a much greater emphasis on linking our theories and measures together. Given the great advances of data collection efforts in recent years, such a task is increasingly feasible. Arguing that all poverty measures have attributes and drawbacks, Iceland, Kenworthy, and Scopilliti recently proclaimed that ‘there is no objectively or scientifically ‘correct’ measure of poverty’ (2005: 7). It is certainly true that each of the various commonly used poverty indicators have limitations. But the tighter we bind poverty indicators to our theories of poverty, the more scientifically rigorous our studies will be. Sharper tests of poverty's effects and determinants improves the science of our field, and furthers Townsend's own decades-long quest for better measures of poverty that illuminate many of the most pressing social problems of our age. ‘. . . the key determinant of poverty is how resources are distributed through policy and that is a function of politics’ (Bradshaw 2000: 69). Townsend consistently recognizes the crucial role of the state in establishing poverty standards that serve its own interest, obfuscating poverty trends (especially when the trend is upward), and excusing the lack of progress in ameliorating poverty (2010 [1962]; 1985). He urges social scientists to investigate these phenomena, while also focusing on the establishment of ‘scientific or objective definitions of poverty’ (1985: 665). Research on the establishment of the US poverty line reveals in detail how the Orshansky thresholds became the official US measure (Fisher 1992), however a comprehensive account of why the US measure has persisted for so long remains wanting. And Townsend's own tireless advocacy on behalf of the disadvantaged – for example, he was a co-founder of the Child Poverty Action Group – pushed policymakers both to measure poverty more accurately and to pass programs aimed at reducing the ranks of the disadvantaged. Yet as the above quote testifies, understanding the linkages between poverty and politics requires going beyond a political sociological analysis of the attempts by policymakers and other interested parties to measure and often excuse poverty. According to Townsend, the state is involved in both generating our needs as well as ‘determining the allocation of resources . . . with which those needs can or will be met’ (1985: 663). This dual process is fundamental to his relative understanding of poverty, for it binds a definition of poverty directly to the particular – and variable – needs of a society and to the resources available at a particular time (2010 [1962]: 85 [210]). Comparative research on welfare state policies' effects on poverty has proliferated in recent years (notable examples include Bäckman 2009; Brady 2005; Kenworthy 1999; Korpi and Palme 1998; Moller et al. 2003). As a result of this work we now have a better understanding of the ways in which policies and other state institutions mediate the relationship between market inequality and poverty. The latter half of Townsend's dual process is now a fertile research area. Research on the former is not quite as far along, especially in studies of poverty in the USA. But how a state – or community – helps generate the needs of its citizens is as critical to our understanding of poverty as is the ways in which the state or community meets its members' needs. Of course defining what needs are decisive in determining full participation in a particular society requires a sophisticated battery of poverty measures only now being utilized in social science research. Is a motor vehicle critical for an individual to participate fully in the life of her community? Or is alternative transportation available? How important is civic participation to an individual's ability to maintain his social relationships, and how will state-sanctioned exclusion from political participation affect his broader ability to engage fully in his community? These are examples of the tough questions the answers to which get us closer to a richer understanding of the complex condition of poverty. They are questions central to Townsend's incredibly influential article. That we have not yet completely answered them speaks not to our own shortcomings, but to the enormous and critically important tasks Townsend assigns to himself and to us all.
